reflection.quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
reflection.reading:
Wednesdays used to be the worst day of the week for me when I was drinking. Not bad enough to be dramatic, not good enough to feel like anything. Just a beige stretch between the weekend that wrecked me and the weekend that would wreck me again. I'd treat it like something to be survived.

Now Wednesdays are where my life actually happens. The gym before work. Coffee that I actually taste. A text from a friend I answer instead of leaving on read for three days. None of it photographs well. None of it would make the highlight reel I once thought sobriety was supposed to deliver. But this is the part I missed for years — that a life isn't built on the big trips or the peak experiences. Whistler seasons and skydives and hunting camps are real, and I have them now, but they're not the point. The point is that I am present for the Wednesday in between.

Durant's line about Aristotle is doing a lot of work here. You don't become someone you can live with on the weekends. You become that person on the ordinary days, doing the small repeatable things — calling your sponsor, eating real food, going to bed at a reasonable hour, showing up. The mountain is built out of Wednesdays.
reflection.meditation: What is one ordinary thing I can show up for today, fully, without needing it to mean anything more than itself?

affirmations: I am building my life on the ordinary days, not just the loud ones.
Today I have the agency to choose what I do next, and that's enough.
I am becoming someone I can sit with in a quiet room.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a Wednesday I get to be awake for. Grateful for the unglamorous parts — the routines, the small texts, the morning coffee that doesn't have to fix anything. Grateful that I no longer have to white-knuckle my way through the middle of the week, and that the people I love can count on me to pick up the phone.
gratitude.closing: One ordinary day at a time.
